worth_it_explanation: Placement and format gaps cost reach and performance without ever showing up as an error. An ad set that supplies only a square asset still serves on Reels and Stories, just with the square locked into a vertical frame, so the asset looks cramped and the delivery system has less surface to optimize against. The checks here are quick. The placements view in Ads Manager shows which surfaces are turned on, and the creative editor shows which placements have a tailored asset. The fixes are configuration and creative changes, not a rebuild, and they widen where the ad can win.
intro: Meta serves a single ad across surfaces with different shapes, including Feed at 4:5, Reels and Stories at 9:16 vertical, and the in-stream and right-column slots. Format coverage is about whether the creative supplied to an ad set fits the placements it can run on, and whether the ad set is even eligible for the high-reach vertical surfaces. These checks confirm that Advantage+ placements is on or turned off for a stated reason, that the ad set supplies a vertical 9:16 asset for Reels and Stories, and that the creative is tailored per placement rather than one crop forced into every frame.

audit_checks.title: Advantage+ placements is turned off in favor of manual placements with no stated reason
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Manual placements narrow where the delivery system can serve, so the ad set competes for a smaller pool of impressions. When the restriction is a deliberate brand or creative decision it can be sound. When it is a leftover default or a copied template, the ad set gives up reach and efficiency for no reason anyone can name.

educate.base:
When you build an ad set in Meta Ads Manager, placements default to Advantage+ placements, which lets Meta serve across its surfaces, including Facebook Feed, Instagram Feed, Reels, Stories, the in-stream and right-column slots, Messenger, and the Audience Network. Switching to manual placements hands that control to you, so the ad set serves only on the surfaces you keep checked.

Manual placements have real uses. A brand may want to stay off the Audience Network for suitability reasons, or run Instagram-only because that is where its audience sits. The setting is a problem when nobody can say why it is on. A manual selection copied from an old ad set, or left from a template, restricts delivery to a subset of surfaces, and the ad set forfeits the reach and the optimization room the wider set would have given it.

Meta frames the choice as a tradeoff between control and delivery flexibility. Its best practices for manual placements describe manual selection as a tool for specific creative or strategic needs, not a default to reach for. A few questions help separate a deliberate restriction from an accidental one:
  • Which placements are excluded, and is there a brand, creative, or measurement reason for each exclusion
  • Was the selection set on purpose for this ad set, or carried over from a duplicated ad set or template
  • Does the excluded surface have a tailored asset that would let it run well if re-enabled
You may want to treat any manual selection without a documented reason as a candidate to re-open to Advantage+ placements, then re-exclude only the surfaces with a real justification.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the high-reach vertical surfaces, Reels and Stories, often carry strong shopping intent, so excluding them through manual placements can cut off volume that converts. You may want to confirm that any surface left off has a reason beyond a copied setting, and that the product creative has a 9:16 version ready if the surface is re-enabled.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation account, manual placements sometimes get set to keep ads off surfaces where the form feels out of context, such as the Audience Network. You may want to confirm each exclusion is deliberate since narrowing placements also narrows the audience the lead form can reach.

learn_more.source_title: Best practices for using manual placements

investigate.intro: Open each ad set's placement setting, note whether it uses Advantage+ placements or manual placements, and for manual ad sets record which surfaces are excluded and why.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open Ads Manager and switch to the ad set level for the campaigns you are auditing.
Open each ad set and find the placements section. Note whether it shows 'Advantage+ placements' or 'Manual placements'.
For any ad set on manual placements, list the surfaces that are unchecked, for example Reels, Stories, or the Audience Network, and check with the account owner whether each exclusion is intentional.
Where a surface is excluded, note whether the ad set supplies an asset shaped for that surface since that affects whether re-enabling it would serve well.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad set, whether placements are set to Advantage+ or manual, and for manual ad sets the excluded surfaces and the stated reason. An ad set on Advantage+ placements, or on manual placements with a documented reason for each exclusion, passes. An ad set on manual placements with no reason given for the restriction is a fail recorded with the ad set name and the excluded surfaces.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the placement setting and any exclusion reasons for each ad set above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more ad sets on {{site_name}} use manual placements with no documented reason for the surfaces they exclude. Manual placements narrow where Meta can serve the ad, so the ad set competes for fewer impressions and gives the delivery system less room to optimize, which can hold back reach and efficiency. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each manual ad set, re-opening it to Advantage+ placements where no brand, creative, or measurement reason supports the restriction, and re-excluding only the surfaces with a justification on record. Before re-enabling a surface, confirm the ad set supplies an asset shaped for it.

audit_checks.title: The ad set supplies no vertical 9:16 asset for Reels and Stories placements
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reels and Stories are full-screen vertical surfaces. An ad set with only a square or landscape asset still serves there, but the asset sits letterboxed or auto-cropped inside the 9:16 frame, which reads as unfinished and uses less of the screen. The placements stay eligible, so the gap shows in how the ad looks and performs rather than as a delivery error.

educate.base:
Reels and Stories render full-screen and vertical, which Meta documents as a 9:16 aspect ratio, while Feed renders closer to square at 4:5. When an ad set is eligible for the vertical surfaces but supplies only a square or landscape asset, Meta still serves it there. It fills the rest of the 9:16 frame with letterboxing or auto-crops the asset to fit, and either result wastes screen and can clip the part of the creative that matters.

The asset that looks finished in Feed can therefore look unfinished in Reels and Stories. A logo near the top edge, or a caption near the bottom, can land under the profile icon or the call-to-action button that these surfaces overlay on the creative. Meta's guidance on aspect ratios across placements recommends a 9:16 asset for the vertical surfaces and a safe zone clear of text and logos at the top and bottom so those overlays do not cover key elements.

Advantage+ creative can generate a 9:16 version from non-vertical media using automatic templates and aspect-ratio variance, which helps, but a template-generated frame is not the same as a vertical asset designed for the surface. A few signs point to a missing vertical asset:
  • The ad set is eligible for Reels or Stories but every uploaded asset is square or landscape
  • The Reels or Stories preview shows letterboxing or a crop that clips a logo, caption, or product
  • Key elements sit in the top or bottom safe zone where the surface overlays its own controls
You may want to add a purpose-built 9:16 asset for the vertical surfaces, or at minimum review the auto-generated preview to confirm nothing important is cropped or covered.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the product is the message, so a square asset auto-cropped into a Reels frame can cut off the product itself or the price overlay. You may want to confirm a 9:16 product asset exists, with the product and any price or offer text kept out of the top and bottom safe zones.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation account, the hook and the offer usually carry the ad, and both are text-heavy, so a non-vertical asset stretched into Stories can push that text under the call-to-action button. You may want to confirm a 9:16 version keeps the headline and offer inside the safe zone where the surface controls do not cover them.

learn_more.source_title: Best practices for aspect ratios across placements
Design requirements for Instagram Stories ads

investigate.intro: Check, per ad set eligible for Reels or Stories, whether it supplies a vertical 9:16 asset and whether the vertical preview crops or covers anything important.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the ad in Ads Manager and confirm the ad set is eligible for Reels or Stories under its placements.
Open the creative and check the uploaded assets. Note whether a 9:16 vertical asset is supplied, or whether only square or landscape media is present.
Switch the preview to the Reels and Stories placements. Look for letterboxing, an auto-crop that clips the subject, or text and logos sitting under the profile icon or the call-to-action button.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad set eligible for the vertical surfaces, whether a 9:16 asset is supplied and whether the vertical preview is clean. An ad set with a 9:16 asset and a preview free of clipping or covered elements passes. An ad set serving on Reels or Stories with only square or landscape media, or with a vertical preview that crops or covers key elements, is a fail recorded with the ad set name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the vertical-asset status and the Reels or Stories preview observation for each eligible ad set above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more ad sets on {{site_name}} serve on Reels and Stories without a vertical 9:16 asset. These surfaces render full-screen and vertical, so a square or landscape asset is letterboxed or auto-cropped, which wastes screen and can clip a logo, caption, or product or push it under the surface's own controls. {{voice_recommend}} adding a purpose-built 9:16 asset for the vertical placements and keeping key elements clear of the top and bottom safe zones. Where a tailored asset is not yet ready, review the auto-generated vertical preview to confirm nothing important is cropped or covered.

audit_checks.title: One aspect ratio is forced across every placement instead of tailoring creative per surface
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Using one crop everywhere is serviceable but leaves performance on the table. Each surface has a shape and a set of overlays it works best with, and placement asset customization lets one ad carry the right crop for each. Skipping it means a compromise frame on most surfaces rather than a fitted one, which is a missed refinement more than a broken setup.

educate.base:
Meta Ads Manager includes placement asset customization, which lets a single ad carry a different image, video, or crop for each placement group, so Feed can show a 4:5 asset while Reels and Stories show a 9:16 one, all within one ad. When an ad skips this and supplies one asset at one aspect ratio, that single crop is stretched, padded, or auto-cropped to fit every surface it runs on.

One crop forced everywhere is rarely wrong, but it is rarely ideal either. The 4:5 Feed asset becomes a letterboxed or cropped frame in the 9:16 vertical surfaces, and a 9:16 asset shrinks inside the wider Feed and in-stream slots. Each surface ends up with a compromise rather than the shape it renders best.

Meta's creative tools for customizing placements describe tailoring media per placement, including different crops and aspect ratios, within one ad set. A few signs point to a single forced aspect ratio:
  • The ad supplies one asset and placement asset customization is not in use
  • The Feed, Reels, and Stories previews all show the same crop, padded or cropped to fit each frame
  • Different surfaces would clearly benefit from a different shape, yet all share one
You may want to consider customizing the creative per placement so each surface gets a fitted asset, starting with the highest-volume surfaces the ad set actually serves on.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the same product shot rarely flatters both a 4:5 Feed slot and a 9:16 Reels frame since one crops the product and the other leaves it small. You may want to consider tailoring the product crop per placement so the item fills each surface without losing the price or offer overlay.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation account, the headline and offer drive the ad, and a single crop pushes that text into different positions on each surface. You may want to consider per-placement crops that keep the headline and call-to-action readable in both the Feed and the vertical surfaces.

learn_more.source_title: Available creative tools for customizing placements
Edit an ad using placement asset customization

investigate.intro: Check, per ad serving across multiple placement groups, whether placement asset customization is in use or whether one asset is forced into every surface.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the ad in Ads Manager and confirm it is eligible for more than one placement group, for example Feed plus Reels and Stories.
In the creative editor, check whether placement asset customization is in use, shown by a tailored asset assigned to a placement group rather than one asset applied to all.
Switch the preview across Feed, Reels, and Stories. If every surface shows the same crop padded or cropped to fit, the ad is forcing one aspect ratio across placements.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad serving across multiple placement groups, whether placement asset customization is in use and whether each surface shows a fitted crop. An ad that tailors the asset per placement, or that runs on a single placement group where one asset is appropriate, passes. An ad serving across Feed and the vertical surfaces with one asset forced into every frame is a fail recorded with the ad name and the surfaces affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether placement asset customization is in use and the per-surface preview observation for each ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more ads on {{site_name}} force a single aspect ratio across every placement instead of tailoring the creative per surface. Each surface has a shape it renders best, so one crop is padded or auto-cropped on most of them, which leaves a compromise frame rather than a fitted one. {{voice_recommend}} using placement asset customization to supply a fitted crop for the high-volume surfaces the ad serves on, for example a 4:5 asset for Feed and a 9:16 asset for Reels and Stories. Start with the placements that carry the most delivery and confirm each preview is clean.
